segments.s045-g89: سرش را is 'his head' with the enclitic possessive -ش and the object marker را (object is fronted here for emphasis). بریده is a past participle/perfect ('cut off') used predicatively. به + زار اهرمن functions as an agent/adjunct: به here means 'by' or 'at the hands of', and زار modifies اهرمن ('the wretched/devilish Ahriman'), so the segment says that Ahriman cut off his head.
segments.s045-g90: تنش را ('his body' with object marker را) is the theme; شده is the verbal adjective/ perfect of شدن 'become' (i.e. 'has become'). کام شیران is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the lions' satisfaction' and کفن ('shroud') is the predicative noun: together the clause means his body became the lions' gratification/food and served as a shroud. The compact noun sequence (کام شیران کفن) is typical poetic apposition where predicates follow شده.
segments.s045-g91: خروشی is a nominal 'a roar' (the -ی forms an abstract noun). به زاری is a prepositional phrase 'with lamentation' (به here = 'in/with' expressing manner). و connects the parallel phrase چشمی پرآب, where چشمی 'an eye' + پرآب 'full of water' (metaphorically 'tear-filled') forms a noun phrase describing the mournful eyes.
segments.s045-g92: ز هر دام و دد uses the poetic preposition ز 'from' meaning 'from/out of' + هر 'every' and the coordinated nouns دام و دد 'snare and enemy'. برده is the past participle of بردن here meaning 'taken (away)'; it governs the following nouns آرام و خواب ('peace and sleep'), so the clause says that peace and sleep were taken from every snare and foe. Note older Persian omits a copula or explicit passive marker; the participle + context yields the passive sense.
segments.s045-g93: سراسر is an adverb meaning 'throughout' that scopes over the following noun phrase; همه intensifies it as 'all/everyone'. کشورش is 'his country' (کشور + possessive suffix -ش). The coordinated noun مرد و زن ('men and women') is the subject being described as present throughout the realm.
segments.s045-g94: به هر جای is a prepositional phrase 'in/to every place' (به here marking location). کرده is the perfect/past participle of کردن used as the main verb 'had made/formed' (poetic word order places it before the object). یکی انجمن literally 'one assembly' indicates they 'formed a single gathering' or 'assembled', with یکی functioning as a numeral/determiner for انجمن.
segments.s045-g95: همه 'all/everyone' applies to the two nominal phrases that follow. دیده پرآب is an adjectival phrase 'eye(s) full of water' (i.e., teary eyes); Persian often uses the singular دِیده generically for eyes. دل پر ز خون uses the poetic preposition ز (= از) 'of/from' to mean 'heart full of blood' (here 'blood' indicates grief or bloodshed).
segments.s045-g96: نشسته is a past-participle/participial adjective 'seated' or 'sitting', describing the people just mentioned. به تیمار is a prepositional phrase 'in care/sorrow' (به marking the state), و links the next phrase, and گرم اندرون is an idiomatic description 'warm/aflame within' (گرم describing their inner state, اندرون = inside), together conveying inward agitation or grief.
segments.s045-g17: This is a locative prepositional phrase: به + noun phrase. به here marks motion/placement into or toward the container ('into the golden coffin'). تابوت زر is a genitive/compound meaning 'golden coffin'. اندرون (postposed) specifies 'inside/within', and پرنیان names the lining/cloth (silk) placed there; the whole phrase sets the location before the verb.
segments.s045-g18: نهاده is a past participle/simple past meaning 'placed/put' with an implied subject ('they'). سر ایرج is a genitive noun phrase 'the head of Irāj' (noun + name). اندر میان is an adverbial phrase 'in the middle / amid', where اندر = 'in/within' and میان = 'middle'. The verb and object relation are thus: (they) placed Irāj's head in the middle.
segments.s045-g19: This preface gives the manner: ابا (here a variant/archaic form related to با) introduces accompaniment; ناله و آه is a coordinated object phrase 'moaning and sighing' (ناله and آه joined by و). با روی زرد is an adjunct 'with a pale face' (با = 'with', روی = 'face', زرد = 'pale/wan'), describing his appearance.
segments.s045-g20: This is the verb phrase + postposed subject: به پیش فریدون = 'into the presence of Fereydun' (به + پیش = 'to/before'); شد is the past verb 'became/happened/approached' used here to mean 'went/approached' and takes the logical subject that follows. آن شوخ مرد is the subject placed after the verb for poetic effect: 'that playful man.'
segments.s045-g21: ز تابوت زر is a prepositional phrase 'from the golden coffin' (ز = 'from' in poetic usage; تابوت زر = 'golden coffin'). تخته is the direct object 'the plank/board' and برداشتند is the past-tense verb 'they removed/took up', third person plural; subject is implied.
segments.s045-g22: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause here ('for/that'); گفتار او = 'his speech/words' (possessive phrase); خوار is an adjective 'despised/worthless' qualifying گفتار; پنداشتند is the past-tense verb 'they thought/imagined' (third-person plural). Together: 'for they thought his words worthless.'
segments.s045-g23: ز تابوت is 'from the coffin' (ز = 'from'); چون here introduces a temporal clause 'when' (classical usage), پرنیان is the silken cloth/covering, and برکشید is the past verb 'they drew up / lifted up'. The clause thus means 'when they lifted the silken covering from the coffin.'
segments.s045-g24: This is the result clause: سر ایرج = 'the head of Irāj'; آمد is an intransitive past verb here meaning 'came/appeared' (was revealed); بریده is a past-participle adjective 'cut off/severed' describing the head; پدید is an adverb/verb 'appeared/was visible' reinforcing آمد. Together: 'the severed head of Irāj came into view.'
segments.s045-g97: همه (everyone) precedes the noun جامه (garment) to mark that everyone is the one doing the action. جامه کرده is a participial construction: جامه (garment) + کرده (past participle of کردن) meaning ‘having put on/garbed (oneself)’. Adjectives کبود و سیاه follow the noun and describe the garments (blue and black). The whole phrase functions as a nominative/group describing the people’s appearance.
segments.s045-g98: نشسته is a past participle used predicatively — ‘seated’ or ‘sitting’ — describing the state of those garbed. به + اندوه forms a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in/with sorrow’ (به here marks the circumstance). در سوگ شاه is another prepositional phrase meaning ‘in mourning (for) the king’, with در introducing the locative/figurative realm ‘in mourning’ and شاه the object of سوگ.
segments.s045-g99: چه مایه is an exclamatory/interrogative phrase meaning ‘what (a) cause/thing’. چنین modifies روز and means ‘such’ (so/like this), placed before روز (‘such a day’). بگذاشتند is the past tense, 3rd person plural of گذاشتن with the archaic prefix ب-; here it means ‘they caused/brought about’ or more literally ‘they set/placed’, completing the question ‘what caused/produced such a day’.
segments.s045-g100: همه is the subject ‘all/everyone’. زندگی (life) is the noun being judged, and مرگ (death) is the predicate/complement: classical Persian often omits a copula or the accusative marker, so the structure reads ‘everyone life death considered’. پنداشتند is the past tense 3rd plural of پنداشتن ‘to suppose/consider/think’, so the clause means ‘everyone considered life to be death.’
segments.s045-g1: This is a participial clause: فریدون (the subject) + نهاده (perfect participle 'having set/placed') + دو دیده (direct object 'two eyes') + به راه (prepositional phrase 'on/toward the road'). The verb نهاده is used like a past participle meaning 'having fixed/placed', so the whole clause means 'Fereydun, having fixed both his eyes on the road'.
segments.s045-g2: This is a coordinated noun phrase: سپاه و کلاه آرزومند شاه = 'the army and the crown (کلاه) that the king desired'. In compressed poetic word order the adjective/phrase آرزومند شاه ('longed-for/by the king') follows کلاه; the ezāfe linking is omitted for metrical reasons, so read it as 'the crown desired by the king.'
segments.s045-g3: چو ('when') introduces a temporal clause: هنگام برگشتن ('the time of returning' — verbal noun برگشتن governed by هنگام) + شاه بود ('it was the king's [time]' or literally 'the king was' with بود as past copula). Together: 'When the time came for the king's return.'
segments.s045-g4: This is a rhetorical question: پدر ('the father') + زان سخن خود ('of that very speech of his' — زان = 'of that', خود = emphatic 'his/own') + کی آگاه بود ('when/how was he aware' — کی used interrogatively here). The clause asks how the father could have known of that utterance.
segments.s045-g5: همی is a habitual/continuous past marker used in poetry; شاه را (object-marked 'for the king') تخت پیروزه ('a throne of turquoise' — noun + adjectival/ornamental epithet) ساخت (past 'made'). Word order puts the object with را before the verb, as is normal in Persian.
segments.s045-g6: Parallel structure to the previous clause: همی (habitual past) + تاج را ('the crown' marked as definite object) + گوهر اندر ('gems within') + نشاخت (an archaic/poetic verb here meaning 'set/inlaid'). In classical verse such an old/rare verb is used; syntactically the gems are placed into the crown and the verb completes the action.
segments.s045-g7: پذیره شدن is a nominal/verbal phrase 'the reception/being received' (پذیره = reception, شدن as verbalizer), را marks it as the object, and بیاراستند is the past plural verb 'they adorned/arranged'. Together: 'They prepared/arranged the reception.'
segments.s045-g8: A simple compound list with final past verb: می‌و (poetic word for wine/drink) و رود (rūd = string instrument/player here) و رامشگران (entertainers/comforters) + خواستند (they desired/called for). In Persian the verb comes at the end and governs the whole coordinated list: 'They brought wine and lute and called the entertainers.'
segments.s045-g49: به روزی is a poetic temporal phrase meaning 'on a day' (به as a preposition introducing a temporal noun). کجا here is an exclamatory/interrogative adverb ('where/what a...') linked directly to the following nominal predicate جشن شاهان; the main verb (an archaic past form) appears at the end of the full sentence. Word order is typical Persian with temporal/adverbial phrase first and the noun phrase (جشن شاهان) before the verb.
segments.s045-g50: وزان پیشتر combines the connective-and + demonstrative 'and from that / and from that time' (وزان) with a temporal adverb 'earlier' (پیشتر). بزمگاهان is the plural noun 'banqueting halls/places of revelry' and again the main verb (بدی) appears at the clause end as an archaic past-tense predicate; the whole segment functions as a temporal/continuative clause linked to the previous one.
segments.s045-g51: This is a nominal phrase built of a proper name followed by appositive epithets: فریدون (proper name) then a sequence of titles/relations. سر functions as an epithet 'head/chief', followed by شاه 'king' and پور 'son/descendant' with جوان qualifying پور. The order (name + apposition + modifiers) is typical for poetic Persian.
segments.s045-g52: بیامد is a simple past tense verb 'he/it came'. The phrase ببر برگرفته is a noun (ببر 'tiger') modified by the past-participle برگرفته ('taken/seized'), forming a participial adjective phrase, and نوان (youthful/new) is an additional epithet. In Persian poetry participial modifiers often follow the noun they qualify; the whole phrase describes the subject introduced in the previous segment.
segments.s045-g53: بر آن تخت شاهنشهی is a prepositional + noun phrase 'upon that imperial throne' (بر = 'on/upon', آن = 'that', تخت = 'throne', شاهنشهی = 'imperial/royal'). بنگرید is the finite verb 'he looked' (archaic/preclassical form with ب- prefix) placed at the end of the clause as is normal in Persian.
segments.s045-g54: سر شاه را is a definite object phrase ('the king's head') marked by the object marker را. نزدر تاج دید places the locative/relational element نزدر ('near/at the presence of') before the noun تاج ('crown'), and دید is the past tense verb 'saw' at the clause end; overall: 'he saw the king's head near/under the crown.'
segments.s045-g55: همان حوض شاهان و سرو سهی is a coordinated nominal phrase: همان 'that/the same' modifies حوض شاهان ('the pool/pond of the kings'), then و connects سرو سهی ('the cypress three/ dark cypress' — سهی is a poetic epithet). Word order is noun + possessor (حوض شاهان = 'pool of the kings').
segments.s045-g56: درخت گلفشان و بید و بهی lists types of trees. درخت 'tree' is followed by the attributive participial/compound adjective گلفشان ('flower-scattering' / flowering), then coordinated elements 'and willow and behi'. Adjectival modifiers (گلفشان) typically follow the noun in poetic Persian; coordination is simple (و).
segments.s045-g25: بیافتاد is a past-tense verb meaning 'fell' (predicate precedes circumstantial phrase here). ز is the poetic preposition 'from' governing اسپ 'horse/steed' which is specified by the name آفریدون (Afrīdūn). به خاک is a goal phrase 'to the ground' with به as 'towards/into' and خاک 'earth/ground'.
segments.s045-g26: سپه 'the host/army' is the subject; the circumstantial phrase سر به سر (literally 'head to head') functions idiomatically as 'one after another' or 'all in turn'. جامه کردند چاک is a causative/periphrastic construction: کردند (they did/made) + چاک (a tear/gash) = 'they rent/they made [their] garments torn.'
segments.s045-g27: This line uses poetic inversion: the predicate (سیه شد / شد) comes before or directly after the noun it governs. سیه شد رخ = 'his face became dark'; و links the coordinated clause دیدگان شد سپید = 'the eyes became white.' شد is the past copula 'became'.
segments.s045-g28: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause. دیدن is the verbal noun (masdar) 'seeing' and functions as the logical subject; دگرگونه is an adjective 'different/other,' and بودش is the past copula with enclitic (بود + ش) meaning 'it was (to him/his)' or simply 'it was.' امید is the predicate noun 'hope.' Together: 'for seeing was a different kind of hope.'
segments.s045-g29: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' خسرو is the subject (proper name). بران‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in that manner' modifying آمد 'came' (past, 3rd sg.). ز راه is a simple prepositional phrase 'from/along the road.'
segments.s045-g30: چنین 'thus/so' points back to the foregoing description. بازگشت here functions as a past verbal form 'he returned.' از پذیره سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the reception/entrance of the host,' with پذیره as 'reception/shelter' and سپاه 'army/host.'
segments.s045-g31: دریده is a past participle 'torn' used adjectivally before درفش 'banner/standard.' نگونسار is an adjective 'mournful/woeful' modifying کوس 'drum/clapper' — two nominal phrases conjoined by و describing the state of the instruments.
segments.s045-g32: رخ نامداران is a genitive-like noun phrase 'the faces of the famed/name-holders' (noun+noun construction common in Persian). به رنگ آبنوس is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in the color of ebony,' with به used adverbially and آبنوس here denoting a very dark/black color rather than literally the wood.
segments.s045-g81: برین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase placed at the clause start meaning ’in this way/thus’ and sets the manner of the action. بگریست is a simple past, 3rd-person singular (he/she wept); it follows the adverb as typical in Persian narrative. چنان is an adverb of degree (‘to that extent’/’so much’) modifying بگریست; together they say he wept so much. بزار here is poetic/elliptical and follows the degree expression to intensify the lament — in context it functions adverbially/verbally to mark the intensity or the effect of the weeping (archaic/poetic usage).
segments.s045-g82: همی is a poetic/progressive particle (here ’still/continually’) accompanying the action that follows. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning ’until’; it links the main clause of intense weeping to the result/time boundary. گیا is a noun ’plant/vegetation’ and is the subject of the subordinate clause; رستش is an archaic perfect/passive-like form (’was restored/was saved’) that predicates about گیا. اندر is the preposition ’in/within’ and کنار is a locative noun ’beside/at the side’, giving the place where the plant was restored.
segments.s045-g83: زمین بستر puts two nouns juxtaposed in a copular/equational sense common in Persian poetry: ’the earth (was) bed’. بستر is the predicate noun following زمین, and و links the parallel phrase that follows. خاک بالین او repeats the same pattern: خاک (earth/soil) followed by بالین (pillow) with او (his) marking possession — word order is noun + predicate-noun + possessor as a compact poetic clause.
segments.s045-g84: شده is a perfect/auxiliary participle meaning ’has become’ and heads the small clause that follows. تیره روشن are two contrasting adjectives (dark / bright) placed together for a poetic oxymoron; their exact scoping is decided by context (his vision/appearance has turned dark though once bright). جهان‌بین is a compound noun/adjective (literally ’world-seeing’ or ’world-wise’) describing the person whose state is being reported. او is the 3rd-person pronoun referring back to the subject — overall: ’his world-seeing (i.e. worldly eye/vision) has become dark’.
segments.s045-g85: در is the locative preposition ’in/at’. بار here (poetic/older usage) names the royal court/assembly or occasion; it follows در to mark location. بسته is an adjective/participial form meaning ’closed/shut’ modifying بار. گشاده‌زبان is a compound adjective ’open-tongued’ (outspoken) describing the speaker; placed after the locative phrase it characterizes her manner as she speaks.
segments.s045-g86: همی is a poetic particle that here accompanies the speech act (presenting the immediate action). گفت is the verbal nucleus meaning ’said’ (past), introducing direct speech. کای is the vocative particle ’O’ used to address someone directly. داور is the addressee noun ’judge/arbiter’ and راستان is an epithet ’righteous/upright’ modifying داور — together forming the vocative ’O righteous Judge’.
segments.s045-g87: کس is an indefinite noun ’someone/anyone’ which with the negated verb نمرد yields the English meaning ’no one died’. از is the preposition ’of/from’ marking the set being referred to; تاجداران is the plural noun ’crown-bearers’ (those who wore the crown). بدین‌سان is an adverbial phrase ’in this way/thus’ modifying نمرد. نمرد is the past negative verbal form here meaning ’did not die’ (the negative sense combines with کس to mean ’no one died’).
segments.s045-g88: که introduces a subordinate clause (’that/which’) giving the specification of the preceding claim. مردست is an archaic/perf. compound (literary contraction of مرده است) meaning ’has died’ — here it states the fact about the named person. این is the demonstrative ’this’ pointing to the person just mentioned; نامبردار is an adjective/noun ’the named/renowned one’ qualifying این. گرد in this syntactic position is a closing particle/poetic tail that rounds the clause in older Persian usage (here it participates in the idiomatic expression referring to the person).
segments.s045-g65: نهاده is a perfect/past participle here meaning 'placed/laid' and precedes its object (سر ایرج); classical Persian often places the verb or participle before the full noun phrase. 'سر ایرج' is a genitive phrase 'the head of Irāj', and 'اندر کنار' is a small prepositional phrase 'at/into the side of, beside', giving the location where the head was placed.
segments.s045-g66: This clause continues with a new action: 'سر خویشتن' = 'his own head' (head + emphatic/reflexive pronoun), followed by کرد, the past of کردن 'to make/do' here meaning 'turned/made (to be)'; the small preposition زی + کردگار is an archaic locative/goal expression ('toward/unto the Creator'). In modern Persian one would expect a different preposition, but in Ferdowsi زی functions as an old-fashioned 'to/toward' before 'Creator'.
segments.s045-g67: همی گفت is a frequentative/imperfective phrase meaning 'he kept saying' or 'he was saying' (همی marks ongoing action). کای is the vocative 'O', and داور دادگر is a compounded address 'judge, the just one' (dāvar = judge, dādgar = just/righteous), forming a direct appeal to the divine arbiter.
segments.s045-g68: بدین (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation) functions as 'thus/with this' introducing the manner; 'بی‌گنه کشته' is a noun phrase 'innocent one slain' (بی‌گنه = innocent, کشته = slain). اندر نگر is an imperative/regardive phrase 'look upon / consider', where نگر is the imperative 'look!'.
segments.s045-g69: به خنجر marks the instrument ('by/with a dagger'), where به can indicate agency/instrument in classical usage. 'سرش کنده' is a passive/resultative phrase 'his head cut off' (سر + pronominal suffix ش = 'his', کنده = 'severed/cut off'). 'در پیش من' is a locative phrase 'in my presence / before me', with پیش 'before/in front of' and من as the clitic-determined pronoun 'me'.
segments.s045-g70: تنش خورده is another resultative cluster: تن 'body' + ش 'his' + خورده (past participle) 'eaten/maimed/tormented'. شیران آن انجمن is a genitive/nominal phrase 'the lions (i.e. brave warriors) of that assembly' where شیران is used metaphorically for valiant men and آن انجمن 'that assembly/gathering' specifies whose lions.
segments.s045-g71: دل هر دو = 'the heart of each/both' (دل = heart; هر دو = both). بیداد here means 'injustice' or the cruel wrong suffered, and از آن سان بسوز is a jussive/optative expression: از آن سان 'from that manner/so much' + بسوز (subjunctive/optative form of سوزیدن) meaning 'may (their hearts) burn' or 'let them burn (with grief/anguish)'. The clause expresses a wish/consequence.
segments.s045-g72: که introduces the result clause 'so that/that', هرگز نبینند is negative-subjunctive 'may they never see' (هرگز = never, نبینند = they do not see / subjunctive they see not), جز تیره‌روز = 'except dark days / nothing but ill‑fate' (جز = except, تیره‌روز = ill‑fated/wretched days).
segments.s045-g33: تبیره سیه کرده is a resultative clause: کرده is the perfect participle/verb meaning 'made' or 'has made' and applies to the preceding noun (تبیره). سیه is attributive 'black/dark' describing what was made; روی پیل is a genitive noun phrase 'the face of the elephant' (روی = face, پیل = elephant) placed after the verb phrase. The conjunctive و simply links this clause to the next phrase.
segments.s045-g34: پراکنده is adjectival/adverbial 'scattered' or 'sprinkled'; the prepositional phrase بر تازی اسپانش ('on/over his Tazi/Arab horses') follows, where اسپانش is اسپان + ش (the possessive clitic 'his') meaning 'his horses'. نیل at the end names what is scattered: 'indigo/blue' (color). Word order is poetic: the scattered thing (نیل) comes after the locative phrase.
segments.s045-g35: پیاده is an adjective 'on foot/infantry' placed before each noun to qualify it: 'the foot-spahbed' (پیاده سپهبد) and 'the foot-army' (پیاده سپاه). This is a compact poetic apposition: two coordinated noun phrases sharing the same adjective repeated for emphasis.
segments.s045-g36: پر از خاک is a prepositional phrase 'full of dust' modifying the previously mentioned troops. سر برگرفتند راه contains two coordinated predicates: سر برگرفتند 'they raised their heads' (سر = head; برگرفتند = they lifted/took up) and راه برگرفتند (implied) 'they took the road' — here برگرفتند governs both 'سر' (raise the head) and 'راه' (take the road). The verb برگرفتند is perfect past plural 'they took/lifted'.
segments.s045-g37: خروشیدن پهلوانان is an action clause with the infinitive/verb خروشیدن 'to roar' used proverbially as a finite action here; پهلوانان is the subject 'the champions'. به درد is an adverbial phrase 'with pain' or 'in pain' modifying how they roared.
segments.s045-g38: کنان is a participial/gerundial adverb 'thus/so doing', linking to the following action. گوشت تن را is a definite object 'the flesh of the body' (گوشت = flesh, تن = body) marked by the object particle را. بران is a contracted prepositional phrase (بر آن = 'upon that' or 'against that') pointing to the target, and رادمرد is the target 'the noble/upright man'. The verb for the tearing/assault is implied by کنان and the prior clause — a common poetic economy where the participle carries the verbive sense.
segments.s045-g39: برین گونه is a frozen demonstrative phrase 'in this manner' formed from بر + این (here written برین for metrical/poetic reasons). گردد is the present/future imperfective 'becomes/shall be' (impersonal/optative here), and به ما is the dative 'for us'. بر سپهر is a locative phrase 'in/on the heavens/sky'. Word order is inverted for poetry: locative and dative follow the verb.
segments.s045-g40: بخواهد + infinitive ربودن forms a future/intention construction 'he will/wants to snatch (away)'. چو here functions temporally 'when/as' and introduces the subordinate clause چو بنمود چهر 'when he showed/displayed his face' — بنمود is a past-form 'he showed' and چهر is the object 'face'. The sequence therefore means 'when, as he showed his face, he will desire to snatch it away.'
segments.s045-g57: تهی (predicate adjective) precedes the verb دید; together تهی دید = “saw ... empty.” دید is simple past (3rd person singular). از marks what the thing is empty of (here آزادگان), and جشنگاه is the place being described (the festal hall). The order is emphatic/poetic: adjective + verb + prepositional complement.
segments.s045-g58: به introduces the target/recipient (here Kiwan = کیوان). برآورده functions as a perfect/past participle sense (“brought/raised upon”); گرد سیاه is a noun phrase (‘black گرد’), where گرد here is a nominal element (literally “a black dust/circle/fate”) modified by سیاه. The whole phrase means that a dark/blight-like thing was brought upon Kiwan.
segments.s045-g59: همی is a frequentative/adverbial particle meaning “kept on / continuously.” سوخت is simple past “burned” and باغ is the affected thing (garden). The second clause parallels the first: همی خست (خست = ‘became tired/worn’) modifies روی (face); the verb/adjective follows the adverbial همی and the affected noun follows it in the poetic word order.
segments.s045-g60: Again همی marks continuous action. ریخت here is the verbal sense ‘to pour/flow’ used with اشک (tears): ریخت اشک = “tears poured/flowed.” In the second half کند موی, کند is past tense (3rd person) of a verb meaning ‘pluck/tear’ (listed senses give a general ‘do/make’); موی is its object. The two clauses are conjoined by و.
segments.s045-g61: میان (here ‘middle’/‘waist’) is the direct object marked by را. به زنار خونین is an instrumental phrase ‘with a bloody girdle/belt’ (به + noun). ببست is past tense (3rd person) with the old prefix ب- (poetic), here meaning ‘he bound/tied’ — so the clause = “he bound his waist with a bloody girdle.”
segments.s045-g62: فکند is past tense ‘he threw/caused’ and takes آتش as its object: فکند آتش = ‘he made/fire/caused fire’ (i.e. set fire). اندر is an archaic preposition ‘in/within’ and سرای (poetic) is the place ‘house/room’; نشست is past ‘he sat/settled.’ The sequence gives three consecutive actions (bound, set fire, sat).
segments.s045-g63: گلستانش is ‘his rose-garden’ (possessive suffix ش), and برکند is past ‘uprooted’ (verb before its object in poetic order). The conjunction و links the second clause: سروان here, in context of a garden, functions as the standing trees (cypresses) or tall ones, and بسوخت is a past passive-like form ‘was burned/was consumed.’
segments.s045-g64: به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase ‘in one moment / suddenly.’ چشم شادی is a genitive construction ‘the eye of joy’ (i.e. eyes of rejoicing), and بدوخت is past (with poetic ب- prefix) of دوخت ‘sewed/stitched.’ Metaphorically here دوخت means the shutting/ending of joyful sight; the verb agrees with the singular grammatical subject چشم.
segments.s045-g73: به here introduces the manner/means ('with' / 'by'); داغی is a noun used figuratively as 'a burning wound/grief'; جگرشان is a possessive compound 'their jigar' (heart/seat of feeling). کنی is 2nd-person singular present of کردن (here causative 'you make') and is followed by the predicative آژده (an adjective 'alarmed/terrified'), so the clause literally says 'you make their heart burned/alarmed'. Word order is typical classical Persian: object/phrase before the verb and the predicative after the verb.
segments.s045-g74: که is the conjunction 'that/so that' introducing the result/purpose clause. بخشایش is 'forgiveness' and آرد (3rd sg. of آوردن) is used optatively/indicatively 'may bring / brings'. بریشان is a contracted prepositional phrase (بر ایشان) meaning 'to/for them'. دده is a noun (a title/name 'the elder') functioning as the agent; classical Persian often places the subject after the verb.
segments.s045-g75: همی is an archaic/adverbial particle adding a continuous or emphatic sense ('still/continually') before the verb. خواهم is 1st person singular present of خواستن and here means 'I ask/wish'. از marks the source ('from'), and روشن کردگار is a noun phrase 'the Radiant/Illuminated Creator' (epithet + God). The whole segment expresses a continuing petition to God.
segments.s045-g76: که introduces the content/purpose clause 'that...'. چنان/چندان here means 'so much/that much' qualifying زمان 'time' (چندان زمان = 'so much time'). یابم is the 1st singular subjunctive/present of یافتن used here as 'I may obtain/attain'. از روزگار marks the source ('from the course of days / the world/fate'), completing the wish.
segments.s045-g77: که is the relative/conjunction 'that'. از تخم ایرج is a genitive phrase 'from the seed/offspring of Irāj' (تخم = 'seed/offspring'). یکی is the indefinite numeral 'one/a certain one' and نامور is the adjective 'renowned' following the noun, so the phrase reads 'that from Irāj's seed a famous one...'.
segments.s045-g78: بیاید is an optative/present form 'may come' or 'may arise'. برین is composed of بر + ین (here orthographic/formal use for این) meaning 'upon/for this' (بر این). کین is 'vengeance/hatred'. ببندد is 3rd sg. present/subjunctive of بستن 'to bind', used idiomatically with کمر ('waist') — کمر بستن means 'to gird oneself' or 'prepare (for action)'. Together: 'may one arise to gird his loins against this vengeance.'
segments.s045-g79: چو here means 'when' (a temporal conjunction). دیدم is the past 1st singular 'I saw'. چنین is 'thus/this way'. زان سپس is a temporal sequence 'after that / thereafter' (زان = 'from that'). شایدم is a colloquial/poetic contraction of شاید + م giving a modal sense 'perhaps / it may be (that)'. The whole segment sets a time and a tentative modal stance.
segments.s045-g80: اگر is the conditional 'if'. خاک بالا literally 'high earth/upper soil' (خاک = earth; بالا = high/above) — an image for some elevated ground or perhaps the realm above. بپیمایدم is 1st singular present-subjunctive of پیمودن with the prefix ب- (بپیمایدم = 'I may traverse/scale' or 'I may accomplish'); the verbal morphology shows the potential/subjunctive mood ('may I/that I might'). The clause finishes the conditional begun by شایدم ('perhaps, if...').
segments.s045-g9: ببردند is the past, 3rd-person plural of بردن ('they carried/took'); تبیره here is the direct object (an archaic/poetic noun — likely the bier) placed before the verb. و is the coordinating conjunction linking the two actions. پیل is the noun 'elephant' followed by the prepositional phrase از درش ('from/at its gate'), where در is the noun 'door/gate' and ش is the enclitic possessive 'his/its'. Word order (object before verb, then linked noun phrase) is typical of classical poetic Persian.
segments.s045-g10: ببستند is the past, 3rd-person plural of بستن and here means 'they fastened/tied/arrayed' (not simply 'shut'); آذین is the object 'decoration/ornament'. به هر کشورش is a locative/target phrase ('on/for each of his countries/realms') where the enclitic ش marks possession. The verb precedes its complement in this poetic ordering but functions as the main action.
segments.s045-g11: به + زین اندرون marks a location ('on/at the saddle, inside') with زین 'saddle' and اندرون 'inside/within'. بود is the past copula 'was/were' (3rd-person) used existentially; the subject شاه و سپاه ('the king and the army/host') follows the verb — an inverted order common in Persian poetry for emphasis.
segments.s045-g12: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one'; گرد تیره functions as a compound meaning a 'dark throng/host' (classical گرد here = band/host, modified by تیره 'dark/ominous'). برآمد is a past verb 'emerged/appeared'; ز راه is the poetic prepositional phrase 'from the road'. The clause order places the indefinite + group before the verb, then the source phrase.
segments.s045-g13: هیونی is a noun 'beast/monster' serving as the subject; برون is the adverb 'forth/out' and precedes the verb آمد ('came') — a normal classical placement. از تیره گرد is a following prepositional phrase 'from the dark throng/host' that gives the source.
segments.s045-g14: نشسته is a participle meaning 'seated' or 'having sat (down)'; برو here functions as an older/periphrastic element meaning roughly 'went on' or 'proceeded' (literally 'went'), so نشسته برو سوگواری conveys 'sat (and) went on mourning'. سوگواری is the noun 'mourning/lament', and به درد ('in/with pain') qualifies the manner — درد here is emotional sorrow. This construction (participle + برو) expresses a continuing or ensuing action.
segments.s045-g15: خروشی is a noun 'a cry/roar/clamor' and is the thing produced; برآورد is the past of برآوردن used here in the sense 'brought forth/raised'. دل سوگوار ('the mourning heart') is the agent/subject, placed after the verb (inverted order typical of poetry).
segments.s045-g16: This short nominal phrase is locative/identifying: یکی 'one/a certain' + زر 'gold' (a piece of gold) followed by تابوتش 'his coffin' (ش = his) and the locative اندر کنار 'at/beside'. The copula is omitted (a common poetic ellipsis), so the sense is 'beside his coffin there was one piece of gold.'
segments.s045-g41: مَبَر is a negative imperative (‘do not take/do not put’). خود is the direct object (‘yourself/one’s self’). به + مهرِ زمانه is a prepositional phrase ‘to/into the favour of the age/world’ and گمان (‘assumption/conjecture’) names the attitude being warned against: ‘do not assume yourself beloved by the world.’ Word order is poetic but the clause means ‘do not place yourself into the belief of the world's favour.’
segments.s045-g42: نه نیکو بود is a negative predication ‘it was not/it is not good.’ راستی is the subject/topic ‘truthness/honesty,’ and در کمان is a locative/metaphorical phrase ‘in the bow’ (here a poetic image for the world’s treacherous frame). The line uses inverted/elliptical order typical of Persian poetry: literally ‘not good was truth in the bow,’ i.e. ‘honesty does not do well in that setting.’
segments.s045-g43: چو is the conditional ‘if/when.’ دشمنش is دشمن + enclitic ش (‘its/him’) referring back to ‘the world’ (زمانه); گیری (from گرفتن) is a 2nd-person form ‘you take/treat (as).’ نمایدت is an older conjugation = نماید + ت giving ‘it shows you’ (the verb presents the world as the subject showing affection). مهر is the object ‘affection/favour.’ The clause therefore reads ‘if you make it your enemy, it will show you favour.’
segments.s045-g44: وگر is ‘and if / but if.’ دوست خوانی is دوست + خوانی (2nd-person present ‘you call/name [someone]’), i.e. ‘if you call it a friend.’ نبینیش is the negative present ‘you do not see it/him’ with the same enclitic ش referring to ‘it/the world.’ چهر ‘face’ is the object of seeing. Together: ‘and if you call it friend, you will not see its face.’
segments.s045-g45: یکی پند is ‘one piece of advice’ (a single admonition). گویم is first-person ‘I say’ (here ‘I will say / I give’); ترا is the indirect object ‘to you’ (enclitic/oblique form), من is an emphatic subject ‘I,’ and درست is an adverb/adjective ‘truly/rightly/plainly.’ Word order is emphatic: ‘One counsel I (plainly) tell you.’ Note: the dictionary senses given for گویم in the metadata refer to the noun 'conversation' and do not match the verb use here.
segments.s045-g46: دل از مهر گیتی is a prepositional phrase ‘the heart from the world’s love’ (i.e. love of the world). ببایدت is a modal construction ‘it is necessary for you / you must’ (notice the enclitic ت ‘you’). شست is the verbal root meaning ‘washed’ used here as the verb ‘wash’ (in classical style the past-stem can function as the action to be done). Together: ‘You must wash the heart of the world’s love away.’
segments.s045-g47: سپه is the subject ‘the host/army.’ داغ دل شاه is a genitive chain ‘the burning grief of the king’s heart’ (داغ = burning sorrow, دل شاه = the king’s heart). با‌های و هوی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ‘with sighs/cries and wailings’ (با = with; هوی here is a poetic word for outcry/passion). The phrase groups the emotional state of the host: ‘the king’s host, burning with grief, with cries and lamentation…’
segments.s045-g48: سوی باغ ایرج is the directional phrase ‘toward Iraj’s garden’ (سوی = toward; باغ ایرج = Iraj’s garden). نهادند is the past-tense transitive verb ‘they placed/set/turned’ (here ‘they turned’). روی is the object ‘face/faces’ (they ‘set/placed their faces toward’ = they turned toward). The clause means ‘they turned their faces toward Iraj’s garden.’

sentences.s045-s45: Ahriman cruelly cut off his head; his body became the lions' satisfaction and a shroud.
sentences.s045-s46: There rose a wailing roar and eyes full of tears; peace and sleep were taken away from every snare and foe.
sentences.s045-s47: All across his country, men and women everywhere had gathered into assemblies.
sentences.s045-s48: All had eyes full of tears and hearts full of blood, sitting in sorrow and inwardly aflame.
sentences.s045-s9: They had laid silken lining inside the golden coffin and placed Irāj’s head in the middle.
sentences.s045-s10: That playful man, with moans and sighs and a pale face, went before Fereydun.
sentences.s045-s11: They took up the plank from the golden coffin, for they deemed his words contemptible.
sentences.s045-s12: When they drew up the silken covering from the coffin, the severed head of Irāj appeared.
sentences.s045-s49: Everyone donned garments of blue and black, sitting in sorrow in mourning for the king.
sentences.s045-s50: What cause brought about such a day? Everyone took life to be death.
sentences.s045-s1: Fereydun fixed both his eyes on the road — upon the army and the crown the king longed for.
sentences.s045-s2: When it was the king's time to return, how could the father have been aware of that speech of his?
sentences.s045-s3: They fashioned for the king a turquoise throne, and they set jewels within the crown.
sentences.s045-s4: They adorned the place of reception; they brought wine and lute, and summoned the entertainers.
sentences.s045-s25: On one day — where there were the kings' celebrations — and from that time earlier there were the banqueting halls' revels.
sentences.s045-s26: Fereydun, the chief of the kings' line and a young son, came — having seized/taken a tiger, youthful.
sentences.s045-s27: He looked upon that imperial throne; he saw the king's head under/near the crown.
sentences.s045-s28: The same kings' pool and the dark cypress, the flowering trees and the willow and the quince.
sentences.s045-s13: He fell from Afrīdūn’s steed to the ground; the host, one and all, rent their garments.
sentences.s045-s14: His countenance darkened and his eyes turned white; for to see (him) was a different kind of hope.
sentences.s045-s15: When Khosrow came along the road in that manner, thus he returned from the army’s reception.
sentences.s045-s16: The standard was torn and the drum mournful; the faces of the famed were the color of ebony.
sentences.s045-s41: He wept in this manner so much that he bewailed until the plants at his side were restored.
sentences.s045-s42: The earth was his bed and the soil his pillow.
sentences.s045-s43: In the closed court, outspoken, she said: ‘O righteous Judge!’
sentences.s045-s44: No one of the crown-bearers died in such a manner as this renowned man has died.
sentences.s045-s33: He laid Irāj’s head at his side, and turned his own head toward the Creator.
sentences.s045-s34: He kept saying, 'O just Judge, behold this innocent one thus slain.'
sentences.s045-s35: With a dagger his head was cut off before me; his body was torn (eaten) by the lions of that assembly.
sentences.s045-s36: May the hearts of the two burn from that injustice so that they never see anything but ill‑fate.
sentences.s045-s17: They blackened the tabireh and the elephant's face, and scattered indigo over his Arabian horses.
sentences.s045-s18: The foot spahbed and the foot army, full of dust, raised their heads and took the road.
sentences.s045-s19: The champions roared in pain; thus (they), tearing at the flesh of the body, fell upon that nobleman.
sentences.s045-s20: Thus will it be for us in the heavens when, as he shows his face, he will wish to snatch it away.
sentences.s045-s29: He saw the festal-hall empty of the nobles; a black fate had been brought upon Kiwan.
sentences.s045-s30: The garden burned and faces grew weary; tears poured and hair was torn out.
sentences.s045-s31: He girded his waist with a bloody belt, set fire within the house, and sat down.
sentences.s045-s32: He uprooted his rose-garden and the cypresses burned; in a single moment the eye of joy was sewn shut.
sentences.s045-s37: Make their hearts burn with such a wound that the elder may bring them forgiveness.
sentences.s045-s38: I continually ask the Radiant Creator that I may obtain so much time from the world.
sentences.s045-s39: That from Irāj’s seed a renowned one may come to gird his loins for this vengeance.
sentences.s045-s40: When I saw this, then perhaps — if I can — I shall scale the high earth.
sentences.s045-s5: They carried the bier and (at) its gate stationed an elephant; they fastened ornaments on each of his realms.
sentences.s045-s6: On the saddle within were the king and his host.
sentences.s045-s7: A beast came forth from the dark host; sitting down, it bewailed in sorrow.
sentences.s045-s8: The mourning heart gave a cry; beside his coffin lay a piece of gold.
sentences.s045-s21: Do not pin your hopes on the world's favour; honesty fares ill in its treachery.
sentences.s045-s22: If you treat it as an enemy it will show you favour; but if you call it a friend you will not see its face.
sentences.s045-s23: I will give you one piece of advice plainly: you must wash the heart of the world’s love away.
sentences.s045-s24: The king’s host, their hearts burning with grief and with cries and lamentations, turned their faces toward Iraj’s garden.
